quiz.title: Gross Domestic Product (GDP)
quiz.subtitle: Unit 2 Quiz 1: Conceptual Foundations of GDP
quiz.subject: Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: First Year - GDP Definition, Exclusions, and Components

questions.question: Which of the following items is NOT counted in the calculation of GDP?
options: The purchase of a newly constructed house.
Social Security benefits to a retiree.
A haircut paid for at a local barbershop.
An increase in business inventories.
questions.explanation: Social Security is a transfer payment. The government redistributes income without receiving a new good or service in return, so no production occurs.

questions.question: To avoid double counting, GDP counts only:
options: Intermediate goods.
Used goods.
Final commodities.
Raw materials.
questions.explanation: GDP measures only the value of final goods and services sold to end users. Counting intermediate goods like steel and then the finished car would count the steel twice.

questions.question: A Canadian citizen works in New York City and earns a salary. How does this affect GDP and GNP?
options: Increases U.S. GDP and U.S. GNP.
Increases Canadian GDP and Canadian GNP.
Increases U.S. GDP and Canadian GNP.
Increases Canadian GDP and U.S. GNP.
questions.explanation: GDP is geographic (produced in the U.S.). GNP is based on citizenship (earned by a Canadian). The income is produced in the U.S. (U.S. GDP) but earned by a Canadian citizen (Canadian GNP).

questions.question: If a household buys a used car from a neighbor, how is GDP affected?
options: GDP increases by the value of the car.
GDP increases only if the price is higher than when new.
GDP does not change.
GDP increases by the commission paid to any middleman (if applicable).
questions.explanation: GDP measures current production. The used car was counted in GDP the year it was originally manufactured. Reselling it is a transfer of an existing asset, not new production.

questions.question: A steel mill sells \$5,000 worth of steel to an automobile manufacturer. The manufacturer uses the steel to build a car and sells the car to a consumer for \$25,000. By how much does GDP increase as a result of these two transactions?
questions.explanation: The steel is an intermediate good. Only the final market value of the car (\$25,000) is counted. Adding the steel and the car would be double counting.

questions.question: Which component of GDP includes the purchase of a new home by a family?
options: Consumption (C)
Investment (I)
Government Spending (G)
Net Exports (X - M)
questions.explanation: In national income accounting, the purchase of a new house is classified as Residential Fixed Investment. It is considered an investment good because it produces a flow of housing services over time.

questions.question: An American company produces laptops in a factory located in Vietnam. The value of these laptops is included in:
options: U.S. GDP only.
Vietnam's GDP only.
Both U.S. GDP and Vietnam's GDP.
Neither country's GDP because it is a multinational corporation.
questions.explanation: GDP is based on the physical location of the factory. Since the factory is located within the geographic borders of Vietnam, the production counts toward Vietnam's GDP.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered an "Investment" (I) in the Expenditure Approach formula?
options: Buying 100 shares of Apple stock.
A company building a new warehouse.
Putting money in a savings account.
Buying a U.S. Treasury Bond.
questions.explanation: In economics, "Investment" means the creation of new physical capital (tools, factories, equipment) or new housing. Financial transactions like stocks and bonds are not counted in GDP.

questions.question: How are goods that are produced but not sold (inventory accumulation) treated in GDP?
options: They are ignored until the year they are sold and there is proof of revenue.
They are counted as part of Investment (I) in the year they are produced.
They are subtracted from GDP as a loss since they are not sold.
They are counted as Government Spending.
questions.explanation: If a good is produced this year but sits in inventory, it is treated as if the company "bought it from itself." This counts as Investment (I) this year. When sold next year, it will be subtracted from that year's investment to avoid double counting.

questions.question: Nominal GDP is calculated using:
options: Current year prices and current year quantities.
Base year prices and current year quantities.
Base year prices and base year quantities.
Current year prices and base year quantities.
questions.explanation: Nominal GDP is the raw monetary value of production at the time it was produced. It has not been adjusted for inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following transactions would be counted in this year's GDP?
options: You pay a teenager down the street \$20 to mow your lawn.
You buy a 1965 Mustang at a classic car auction.
You receive a \$1,000 check from the government for disaster relief.
You buy a newly constructed condo on the beach.
questions.explanation: New construction is current production and counts in GDP. The car is a used good. The disaster check is a transfer payment. The lawn mowing is a service but is typically unreported (underground economy) and therefore not captured in official GDP statistics.

questions.question: A U.S. citizen buys a bottle of French wine at a restaurant in Miami. What happens to U.S. GDP?
options: Consumption (C) increases; Net Exports (X-M) decrease.
Consumption (C) decreases; Investment (I) increases.
Net Exports (X-M) increase; Consumption (C) stays the same.
Only Imports increase; there is no effect on total GDP.
questions.explanation: The purchase increases Consumption (C). However, because the wine was imported, Imports (M) also increase. In the formula C + I + G + (X - M), the C goes up but the -M also goes up, canceling out the effect on total GDP.

questions.question: Which of the following is the largest component of U.S. GDP?
options: Government Spending (G)
Investment Spending (I)
Net Exports (X - M)
Personal Consumption Expenditures (C)
questions.explanation: Consumer spending on goods (durable/nondurable) and services drives the vast majority of economic activity in the United States, typically comprising around 68-70% of GDP.

questions.question: The Income Approach to calculating GDP includes all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Wages and Salaries.
Rental Income.
Corporate Profits.
Transfer Payments.
questions.explanation: The Income Approach adds up all the income earned from producing goods: Wages (Labor), Rent (Land), Interest (Capital), and Profit (Entrepreneurship). Transfer payments are not earned income from current production.

questions.question: Economists distinguish real GDP from nominal GDP to
options: determine whether economic welfare has changed.
determine whether real production has changed.
measure the change in nominal interest rates.
determine whether the government sector is growing.
questions.explanation: Real GDP adjusts for price changes (inflation) to isolate changes in actual output. Nominal GDP can rise simply due to higher prices, so economists use real GDP to measure true production changes.

questions.question: ___________ is the total market value of all final goods and services produced by the citizens and businesses of a country in a given period, regardless of where that production occurs geographically.
options: Gross Domestic Product
Gross National Product
National Income
Net Domestic Product
questions.explanation: Gross National Product (GNP) measures production based on citizenship/ownership, regardless of geographic location. GDP measures production within borders.

questions.question: ______________ is the sum of all payments to the factors of production; i.e., the sum of all wages, rent, interest and profit.
options: Gross Domestic Product
Gross National Product
National Income
Net Domestic Product
questions.explanation: National Income (NI) represents the total earnings of all factors of production (labor, land, capital, and entrepreneurship) within an economy.

questions.question: Which of the following might indicate inflation?
options: Nominal GDP greater than Real GDP
Real GDP greater than Nominal GDP
Nominal GDP and Real GDP increase at the same rate
Nominal GDP and Real GDP decrease at the same rate
questions.explanation: When Nominal GDP exceeds Real GDP, it indicates that prices have risen (inflation). Real GDP holds prices constant at base-year levels, so any difference reflects changes in the price level.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have a strong conceptual understanding of what GDP includes, what it excludes, and how its components are classified.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Review the treatment of used goods, transfer payments, and the geographic vs. citizenship distinction between GDP and GNP.
scoreMessages.50: Solid foundation. Focus on the key exclusions from GDP: intermediate goods, used goods, and transfer payments.
scoreMessages.0: Keep studying. Remember: GDP measures current domestic production. If no new good or service is created, it's not in GDP.
